query: adverse effects of long-term adrenocorticosteroid use

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia -- Treatment / Management -- Long-Term Management
retrieved_docs.content: Plasma renin activity levels are useful for monitoring the effectiveness of mineralocorticoid and sodium replacement. Hypotension, hyperkalemia, and elevated renin levels indicate the need for a dose increase, while hypertension, tachycardia, suppressed plasma renin activity, and hypokalemia suggest overtreatment. Adequate fludrocortisone replacement reduces steroid doses by lowering ACTH drive caused by hypovolemia and arginine vasopressin release. However, excessive fludrocortisone dosing may impair growth.

retrieved_docs.title: Corticosteroid Adverse Effects -- Mechanism of Action -- Mineralcorticoid Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoids bind to mineralocorticoid receptors (MRs) and produce their mineralocorticoid effect (i.e., increasing sodium and decreasing potassium), but only when used at the high dose and for an extended period.

retrieved_docs.title: Corticosteroid Adverse Effects -- Indications -- As a Replacement Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenocortical insufficiency (Addison disease) Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH)

retrieved_docs.title: Corticosteroid Adverse Effects -- Adverse Effects -- Cardiovascular Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Mineralocorticoid effects, especially as seen with cortisol and cortisone, can lead to fluid retention, edema, weight gain, hypertension, and arrhythmias by increasing renal excretion of potassium, calcium, and phosphate. Hypertension usually occurs with higher doses only. [11] Long-term use of medium-high dose glucocorticoids has implications in premature atherosclerosis in a dose-dependent pattern. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia -- Treatment / Management -- Long-Term Management
retrieved_docs.content: In other rare variants, monitoring may include other relevant metabolites, including: With excessive mineralocorticoid activity, DOC levels should be measured. With an 11β-OH deficiency, 11-deoxycortisol levels should be measured. With 3β-HSD-2 defects, pregnenolone, 17-hydroxypregnenolone, and DHEA levels should be measured. Once growth is complete in older children and adolescents, treatment may involve prednisone (5-7.5 mg daily in 2 equally divided doses) or once-daily dexamethasone (0.25-0.5 mg). In adults, in addition to the aforementioned hormonal and clinical monitoring, annual assessments should include blood pressure, body mass index (BMI), and evaluation for Cushingoid features. Mineralocorticoid replacement: Mineralocorticoid replacement is a cornerstone of managing salt-wasting CAH to address sodium loss, prevent adrenal crises, and optimize growth and development.

retrieved_docs.title: 21-Hydroxylase Deficiency -- Treatment / Management -- Long-Term Management
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocortisone is the preferred treatment due to its short half-life and minimal impact on growth suppression. Treatment efficacy is best assessed by monitoring morning ACTH, 17-hydroxyprogesterone, dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate, and androstenedione. Although 17-hydroxyprogesterone levels are still higher than normal, a target range of 500 to 1000 ng/dL helps avoid the adverse effects of overtreatment. Once the initial management plan is implemented with close monitoring, monitoring frequency should be every 3 months until 18 months and then every 4 months from 18 months until growth is complete. Children should also have an annual bone age radiograph and careful monitoring of linear growth. Older children and adolescents, where growth is complete, may be treated with prednisone (5 to 7.5 mg daily in 2 divided doses) or once-daily dexamethasone (0.25 to 0.5 mg).

retrieved_docs.title: Hypercortisolism -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In the setting where HCM is due to exogenous glucocorticoid use, the central management strategy is a carefully monitored reduction in glucocorticoid use with the ultimate aim of total withdrawal of their use. Depending on the initial indication/reason for their use, the duration, and dose of the glucocorticoids and evidence of secondary cortical atrophy, this may or may not be possible. Exogenous HCM can present the managing clinician with the conundrum of a patient with evidence of HCM who concomitantly has secondary adrenal insufficiency from cortical atrophy, which can become clinically apparent following the withdrawal of glucocorticoid therapy is entirely withdrawn. Serial monitoring of serum DHEA-S and ACTH levels, as well as serial ACTH (cosyntropin) stimulation tests, may be required in these sorts of patients over time while reducing the glucocorticoid dose to the closet dose to physiologic replacement doses required to prevent symptoms and signs of adrenal insufficiency. For some such patients, the secondary adrenocortical atrophy may be permanent, and such patients may require long term/life term physiologic adrenocortical replacement therapy with hydrocortisone (or other equivalent glucocorticoids) with adjunctive mineralocorticoid repletion (typically with fludrocortisone). For other patients, the medical indication for their glucocorticoid use such as rheumatologic or pulmonary inflammatory states may prevent weaning off these medications, and the strategy in those cases becomes weaning to the lowest required dose of glucocorticoids to maintain therapeutic effect with concurrent management of any consequent associated metabolic and clinical effects of the long term persistent HCM.

retrieved_docs.title: Corticosteroid Adverse Effects -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids are hormone mediators produced by the cortex of adrenal glands that further categorize into glucocorticoids, mineralocorticoids, and androgenic sex hormones. They are used in a plethora of conditions, commonly called steroid-responsive disorders and dermatoses. Corticosteroids constitute a double-edged sword - significant benefit with a low incidence of adverse effects can be expected if used in proper dosage and for a limited duration; however, wrong dose and/or duration and unmindful withdrawal after prolonged administration can have catastrophic effects. Corticosteroids are used across all medical specialties. This activity reviews the must-know properties of this group of drugs, their broad indications and contraindications, ways of administration, adverse event profile, practical aspects of the pharmacokinetics of different molecules, monitoring essentials, approach to maximize the benefit and minimize adverse effects, and clinically relevant drug-interactions pertinent for all specialists whether used in isolation or administered by an interprofessional team.

retrieved_docs.title: Corticosteroid Adverse Effects -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Several preparations of glucocorticoids are available, each with varying efficacy. Dexamethasone and betamethasone are long-acting with the highest glucocorticoid efficacy with a biological half-life of 36 to 54 hours. Cortisone and cortisol are short-acting with a biological half-life of under 12 hours and are not frequently used. Prednisone, prednisolone, methylprednisolone, and triamcinolone are intermediate-acting with a biological half-life of 18 to 36 hours. The glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid effects of each available preparation vary, with cortisol and cortisone having almost 1 to 1 glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid effects while all others with almost no mineralocorticoid effects. Equivalent glucocorticoid doses can be calculated for these various preparations. 5 mg of prednisone is equivalent in its glucocorticoid effects to 5 mg of prednisolone, 4 mg of methylprednisolone, 4 mg of triamcinolone, 0.75 mg of dexamethasone, 0.60 mg of betamethasone, 20 mg of cortisol, and 25 mg of cortisone.

retrieved_docs.title: Corticosteroid Adverse Effects -- Mechanism of Action -- Effects on the Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal (HPA) Axis
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoids exert negative feedback effects on the HPA axis. They directly suppress adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) and corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) secretion. Additionally, by suppressing the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines that stimulate ACTH and CRP secretion, glucocorticoids further suppress ACTH and CRH secretion indirectly in inflammatory diseases. Chronic HPA axis suppression by glucocorticoids leads to functional adrenal atrophy (sparing the mineralocorticoid producing outer adrenal cortex that is functionally independent of ACTH). The risk of this functional adrenal atrophy and insufficiency is challenging to predict and varies from patient to patient but is largely dependant on the dose and duration of glucocorticoid therapy. The adrenal function generally recovers by slow tapering of glucocorticoids.

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia -- Treatment / Management -- Long-Term Management
retrieved_docs.content: Mineralocorticoid excess: In CAH with excessive mineralocorticoid activity, along with adequate cortisol replacement, antihypertensive measures may be necessary. Aldosterone blockade, such as spironolactone in 11β-OH defects, can help manage both hypertension and excessive androgen levels.

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia -- Treatment / Management -- Long-Term Management
retrieved_docs.content: The goals of therapy are as follows: Treatment aims to provide adequate glucocorticoid replacement during infancy and childhood to prevent adrenal crises, limit early virilization, support normal growth, and ensure sufficient mineralocorticoid levels (and salt, if needed) to prevent electrolyte imbalances and dehydration. In adolescents and adults, the focus of the treatment shifts to achieving normal reproductive function and fertility while avoiding chronic complications of medication insufficiency or excess, including Cushing syndrome. To minimize exposure to glucocorticoids in adulthood and reduce the adverse outcomes associated with supraphysiological steroid doses, control of adrenal-derived androgens is typically relaxed after adult height is reached. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Corticosteroids -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: The toxicity of corticosteroids accounts for one of the most common causes of iatrogenic illness in patients on chronic therapy.  No specific reversal agent exists for corticosteroids. Their effect in excess is manageable by gradual taper and addressing the particular complication (e.g., hyperglycemia, infection, hypertension). If steroid therapy is in place for less than one week, patients can usually stop without tapering. For dosing lasting one to three weeks, tapering should depend on the clinical conditions for which the patient took the medication. Rapid and complete withdrawal can lead to adrenocorticotropic hormone suppression and flare of the underlying disease. Courses over three weeks should have a quick taper to physiologic doses, then slow weaning should follow while evaluating the adrenal function. Long-term, high-dose suppressive therapy can lead to suppression of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis for as long as nine to twelve months following withdrawal. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: 21-Hydroxylase Deficiency -- Treatment / Management -- Long-Term Management
retrieved_docs.content: Plasma renin activity levels may be used to monitor the effectiveness of mineralocorticoid and sodium replacement. Hypotension, hyperkalemia, and elevated renin levels suggest the need to increase the dose, whereas hypertension, tachycardia, suppressed plasma renin activity, and hypokalemia production are signs of overtreatment. Adequate replacement with fludrocortisone helps to reduce steroid doses, as it reduces ACTH drive stimulated by hypovolemia and arginine vasopressin release, but excessive increase in fludrocortisone dosage may retard growth.

retrieved_docs.title: Corticosteroids -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: 9. Cardiovascular adverse effects: Corticosteroid use is associated with hypertension, hyperglycemia, obesity, and conflicting evidence exists regarding hyperlipidemia. [2] Mineralocorticoid activity, which varies by corticosteroid, leads to the retention of free water and sodium with potassium excretion. [2] Studies have found that corticosteroid use is associated with higher rates of cardiovascular events, new-onset atrial fibrillation and flutter, heart failure, and ischemic heart disease. [2] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia -- Treatment / Management -- Long-Term Management
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoid replacement: Glucocorticoid replacement in CAH is essential for managing adrenal insufficiency, controlling androgen excess, and promoting normal growth and development. Treatment is tailored to the specific variant and age of the patient. Cortisol replacement: Hydrocortisone is the preferred treatment due to its short half-life and minimal impact on growth suppression. Oral hydrocortisone is administered in 3 equally divided doses of 10 to 20 mg/m 2 /d, as patients with classic 21-OH deficiency (virilizing CAH) require supraphysiological doses of long-term glucocorticoid treatment to inhibit excessive secretion of CRH and ACTH, as well as to reduce the abnormally high serum concentrations of adrenal androgens. In under-virilizing CAH, physiological doses of hydrocortisone are usually sufficient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1536
retrieved_docs.content: Finerenone, a non‑steroidal mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist, has a contraindicated mechanism of action when used with Cytochrome P450 3A4 Inhibitors [MoA]; co‑administration can impair finerenone metabolism, leading to increased drug exposure and heightened risk of adverse effects, so clinicians should avoid combining these agents or adjust therapy accordingly.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 479
retrieved_docs.content: Mineralocorticoids for systemic use, plain represent a class of mineralocorticoid agents that includes aldosteron, deoxykortikosteron, and fludrokortison. Deoxykortikosteron is linked to tetrahydrodeoxycorticosterone through a mapping relationship, indicating that it is derived from or closely related to that compound. The class is also associated with the term mineralokortikoidy, underscoring its identity as a group of mineralocorticoid substances. These agents act on mineralocorticoid receptors to promote sodium retention and potassium excretion, making them therapeutically relevant for conditions such as adrenal insufficiency and disorders of electrolyte balance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1377
retrieved_docs.content: The plain systemic mineralocorticoid preparation, identified as “Mineralocorticoids for systemic use, plain,” includes fludrokortison as a member and is classified within the mineralokortikoidy (mineralocorticoid) drug group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 506
retrieved_docs.content: Anticorticosteroids are classified as antiadrenal preparations. Antiadrenal preparations belong to the broader category CORTICOSTEROIDS FOR SYSTEMIC USE. Within this hierarchy, the specific class “corticosteroids for systemic use, plain” is a direct subclass of CORTICOSTEROIDS FOR SYSTEMIC USE. Mineralocorticoids for systemic use, plain are a subclass of corticosteroids for systemic use, plain, indicating that mineralocorticoids are a type of systemic corticosteroid. The drug fludrokortison is an example of a mineralocorticoid used systemically and therefore a member of the class Mineralocorticoids for systemic use, plain. The term mineralokortikoidy is linked to Mineralocorticoids for systemic use, plain as a synonymous or related descriptor. This hierarchical organization clarifies how these corticosteroid‑related agents are interrelated, supporting accurate classification, therapeutic decision‑making, and consistent terminology in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1242
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocortisone sodium succinate, a fast‑acting glucocorticoid formulation, is employed in the therapeutic management of both حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة (a hyperplastic or proliferative state) and الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse). In hyperplastic conditions, the anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive actions of hydrocortisone sodium succinate help to reduce excessive cellular proliferation and associated tissue edema. In the setting of circulatory collapse, the drug’s mineralocorticoid activity supports vascular tone and sodium retention, thereby contributing to the restoration of hemodynamic stability. Consequently, hydrocortisone sodium succinate serves as a critical intervention for patients presenting with these severe physiological disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 804
retrieved_docs.content: Mineralocorticoids for systemic use, plain is a preparation that incorporates both aldosterone and fludrokortison as its constituent agents, and each of these agents is a member of the mineralocorticoid class, referred to as mineralokortikoidy. This indicates that the plain systemic mineralocorticoid formulation is composed of these specific mineralocorticoid compounds.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 110
retrieved_docs.content: The adrenal and gonadal hormone group includes aldosterone, deoxycorticosterone (deoxykortikosteron) and a generic endocrine substance, NOS, each listed as a child of this hormone category. Aldosterone and deoxycorticosterone are classified within mineralocorticoids for systemic use, plain, indicating that they are components of plain mineralocorticoid preparations. Deoxycorticosterone is chemically derived from tetrahydrodeoxycorticosterone. The plain mineralocorticoid preparation is a subclass of corticosteroids for systemic use, plain, and is specifically linked to the agent fludrocortisone; it is also described by the term mineralokortikoidy. Corticosteroids for systemic use belong to the broader class of systemic hormonal preparations that exclude sex hormones and insulins, a classification that also includes calcium homeostasis (substance). Anticorticosteroids are defined as anti‑adrenal preparations, and corticosteroids for systemic use are inversely related to anti‑adrenal preparations, reflecting overlapping therapeutic categories. Additionally, the adrenal medulla hormone substance contains the generic endocrine substance, NOS, as a child. This interconnected hierarchy clarifies how individual adrenal hormones, their pharmaceutical formulations, and larger hormonal categories relate to one another, supporting accurate classification, therapeutic decision‑making, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 222
retrieved_docs.content: a potassium‑sparing diuretic can be paired with a loop diuretic (bumetanide) to achieve balanced diuretic therapy while preserving serum potassium levels. Finally, fludrokortison is another member of the Mineralocorticoids for systemic use, plain group, highlighting its role as a synthetic mineralocorticoid used in clinical practice. Together, these entities illustrate the interconnected hierarchy of adrenal hormones, their synthetic analogues, and the pharmacologic agents that modulate their activity, emphasizing the importance of understanding both hormonal pathways and the specific diuretic therapies that target aldosterone‑mediated sodium and potassium balance in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1846
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a class of corticosteroid drugs that exert broad anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive actions throughout the body. Both prednison and rimexolone are individual agents that belong to this systemic glucocorticoid class, sharing the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoid receptor activation, suppression of cytokine production, and modulation of immune cell function. Recognizing prednison and rimexolone as members of the systemic glucocorticoid group helps clinicians understand their therapeutic indications, dosing considerations, and potential side‑effect profiles, which are characteristic of systemic glucocorticoid therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1601
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocortisone sodium succinate is a rapid‑acting glucocorticoid formulation that can be employed in the management of الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), providing anti‑inflammatory and hemodynamic support to help stabilize patients experiencing severe cardiovascular failure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2933
retrieved_docs.content: Androgeny and the class of compounds known as androgens are both classified as forms of nandrolone, a synthetic anabolic‑androgenic steroid. As variants of nandrolone, they share the characteristic hormonal activity of this compound, which includes promoting protein synthesis, increasing muscle mass, and exerting androgenic effects. Recognizing androgeny and androgens as types of nandrolone helps clarify their pharmacologic profile and informs clinical decisions regarding their therapeutic use, potential side‑effects, and regulatory considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1329
retrieved_docs.content: ADRENAL AND GONADAL HORMONES comprise a family of steroid hormones that includes aldosterone and deoxycorticosterone as subordinate members. Aldosterone functions as a key mineralocorticoid that regulates sodium and water balance, thereby influencing blood pressure and extracellular fluid volume. Deoxycorticosterone serves as a biosynthetic precursor in steroidogenesis and also exhibits mineralocorticoid activity, contributing to electrolyte homeostasis. Recognizing that both aldosterone and deoxycorticosterone are child hormones of the broader ADRENAL AND GONADAL HORMONES group aids clinicians in understanding their integrated roles in endocrine regulation and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1245
retrieved_docs.content: Mineralocorticoids for systemic use, plain is a therapeutic class that includes the agents deoxykortikosteron and fludrokortison as its members. Deoxykortikosteron is chemically derived from, or mapped from, tetrahydrodeoxycorticosterone, indicating a close structural relationship between these two steroids. The class Mineralocorticoids for systemic use, plain is linked to the broader category of mineralocorticoids (referred to as mineralokortikoidy), underscoring that both deoxykortikosteron and fludrokortison function as mineralocorticoid drugs used systemically in their plain (uncombined) form. This classification highlights the pharmacological grouping of these compounds within mineralocorticoid therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1537
retrieved_docs.content: ADRENAL AND GONADAL HORMONES is a parent category that includes both aldosteron and deoxykortikosteron as child entities. Aldosteron, deoxykortikosteron, and fludrokortison are each identified as members of the pharmaceutical grouping Mineralocorticoids for systemic use, plain, which is further linked to the term mineralokortikoidy. In addition, deoxykortikosteron is mapped from tetrahydrodeoxycorticosterone, indicating a biochemical derivation. Together, these relationships illustrate that the mineralocorticoid drug class Mineralocorticoids for systemic use, plain encompasses several adrenal hormones—aldosteron, deoxykortikosteron, and fludrokortison—and is associated with the broader concept mineralokortikoidy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 587
retrieved_docs.content: Anticorticosteroids are classified as antiadrenal preparations, indicating that they act to oppose adrenal hormone activity. Anti‑adrenal preparations themselves are a subset of the broader therapeutic class termed CORTICOSTEROIDS FOR SYSTEMIC USE, which encompasses all corticosteroid agents administered systemically. Within this overarching category, a more specific formulation exists called “corticosteroids for systemic use, plain,” representing a plain (non‑combination) version of systemic corticosteroid therapy. This hierarchical organization—Anticorticosteroids → antiadrenal preparations → CORTICOSTEROIDS FOR SYSTEMIC USE, with “corticosteroids for systemic use, plain” as a distinct subclass—clarifies how these drug groups relate to one another and aids clinicians in selecting and categorizing systemic corticosteroid treatments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 393
retrieved_docs.content: ADRENAL AND GONADAL HORMONES is a broad category that includes several specific endocrine substances, among them aldosterone, deoxykortikosteron, and the generic label Endocrine substance, NOS. Both aldosterone and deoxykortikosteron are classified as members of the drug group Mineralocorticoids for systemic use, plain, indicating that they are used therapeutically as mineralocorticoid agents. Fludrokortison is another member of this same mineralocorticoid class, reinforcing the therapeutic focus of the group. The relationship between Mineralocorticoids for systemic use, plain and the term mineralokortikoidy reflects a linguistic or nomenclatural link, essentially denoting the same class of mineralocorticoid drugs. Deoxykortikosteron is directly mapped from tetrahydrodeoxycorticosterone, suggesting that the two terms refer to the same chemical entity or that one is a synonym of the other. Endocrine substance, NOS appears as a child concept under both ADRENAL AND GONADAL HORMONES and Adrenal medulla hormone (substance), highlighting its placement at the intersection of adrenal and gonadal hormone biology and adrenal medullary secretions. Together, these relationships delineate a hierarchical structure in which specific mineralocorticoid hormones and their pharmaceutical forms are organized under broader endocrine categories, facilitating a clear understanding of their biological origins, clinical uses, and terminological connections.
